name: Oni Full
description: Oni are fearsome ogre-like creatures from Japanese folklore, often depicted as powerful demons or devils. They are known for their grotesque appearance, typically possessing sharp claws, wild hair, and one or more horns growing from their heads. Their skin is usually red, blue, green, black, or yellow. Oni are often associated with chaos, destruction, and misfortune, and are frequently portrayed wielding iron clubs or kanabō. While often malevolent, some stories depict Oni as guardians or protectors, albeit fearsome ones. They inhabit remote mountainous regions or hidden realms, and are believed to possess immense strength and magical abilities. Oni are a recurring motif in Japanese art, literature, and theater, embodying both terrifying power and the potential for redemption or transformation. They are a symbol of untamed nature and the darker aspects of human nature, serving as a cautionary figure against greed, anger, and other vices. The portrayal of Oni has evolved over time, with some modern interpretations depicting them as more complex and nuanced characters.
subtitle: Fearsome Ogre-Demons of Japanese Lore
appearance: Oni are typically depicted as large, muscular humanoids with skin that ranges in color from red, blue, green, black, or yellow. They have wild, unkempt hair and one or more sharp horns protruding from their heads. Their faces are often contorted into grotesque expressions, with sharp teeth and menacing eyes. They possess long, sharp claws and often wear loincloths or animal skins. Some Oni carry iron clubs (kanabō) as weapons.
habitat: Oni are said to inhabit remote mountainous regions, caves, and hidden realms. They are often associated with places of darkness and chaos, far from the human world. Some tales depict them living in the underworld or other supernatural dimensions.
behavior: Oni are generally portrayed as malevolent and destructive beings. They are known for their cruelty, greed, and love of causing chaos. They often prey on humans, stealing their possessions and sometimes even consuming them. However, some Oni are depicted as guardians or protectors, fiercely loyal to those they deem worthy. Their behavior can be unpredictable and driven by their primal instincts.

keyMyths.title: Setsubun and the Oni
keyMyths.description: During Setsubun, people throw roasted soybeans while chanting "Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi!" (Demons out! Fortune in!) to drive away evil spirits and welcome good luck. This ritual is a symbolic act of purification and renewal, banishing the Oni from the home.

sources.author: Lafcadio Hearn
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, including tales featuring Oni.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese ghost stories and folklore, including tales featuring Oni.

sources.author: Donald Richie

sources.description: Provides cultural context and insights into the role of Oni in Japanese society and art.
